Ayahs

سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١)
Whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَخْرَجَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ لِأَوَّلِ ٱلْحَشْرِ ۚ مَا ظَنَنتُمْ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ ۖ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُم مَّانِعَتُهُمْ حُصُونُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ فَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَمْ يَحْتَسِبُوا۟ ۖ وَقَذَفَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلرُّعْبَ ۚ يُخْرِبُونَ بُيُوتَهُم بِأَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَيْدِى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ فَٱعْتَبِرُوا۟ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (٢)
It is He who expelled the faithless belonging to the People of the Book from their homes at the outset of [their] en masse banishment. You did not think that they would go out, and they thought their fortresses would protect them from Allah. But Allah came at them from whence they did not suppose and He cast terror into their hearts. They demolish their houses with their own hands and the hands of the faithful. So take lesson, O you who have insight! (2)
وَلَوْلَآ أَن كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْجَلَآءَ لَعَذَّبَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابُ ٱلنَّارِ (٣)
If Allah had not ordained banishment for them, He would have surely punished them in this world, and in the Hereafter, there is the punishment of the Fire for them. (3)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَآقُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۖ وَمَن يُشَآقِّ ٱللَّهَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٤)
That is because they defied Allah and His Apostle; and whoever defies Allah, Allah is indeed severe in retribution. (4)
مَا قَطَعْتُم مِّن لِّينَةٍ أَوْ تَرَكْتُمُوهَا قَآئِمَةً عَلَىٰٓ أُصُولِهَا فَبِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيُخْزِىَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٥)
Whatever palm trees you cut down or left standing on their roots, it was by Allah’s will and in order that He may disgrace the transgressors. (5)

Contents of Surah Al-Hashr

This Surah, which primarily focuses on the story of the Muslims' struggle against a group of Jews known as the "Banu Nadir"—a conflict that ultimately led to their total expulsion from Medina and the cleansing of this holy land—is among the significant, awakening, and soul-stirring Surahs of the Holy Quran. It bears a very close correlation with the final verses of the preceding Surah, where victory was promised to the "Party of Allah" (Hizbullah), and in fact, this event serves as a clear example of that victory.

The content of this Surah can be summarized into six sections:

In the "First Section," which consists of only one verse and serves as an introduction to the various topics of this Surah, there is mention of the universal glorification and sanctification of all beings before Allah, the Almighty and All-Wise.

In the "Second Section," spanning from verse 2 to verse 10 (a total of 9 verses), the story of the Muslims' confrontation with the treaty-breaking Jews of Medina is recounted.

In the "Third Section," comprising verses 11 to 17, the story of the hypocrites of Medina is mentioned, who collaborated closely with the Jews in this agenda.

The "Fourth Section," which consists of only a few verses, contains a series of general exhortations and advice for the general body of Muslims, serving in reality as a conclusion drawn from the aforementioned events.

The "Fifth Section," which is only one verse (verse 21), is an eloquent description of the Holy Quran and an explanation of its impact on the purification of the soul and spirit.

And finally, in the "Sixth Section," which is the final part of this Surah, starting from verse 22 and ending at verse 24, a significant portion of the attributes of Allah’s Beauty and Majesty and His Beautiful Names (Asma al-Husna) are enumerated, which greatly assist man on the path of knowing Allah (Ma'rifat Allah).

Additionally, the name of this Surah is derived from its second verse, which mentions the "Hashr," meaning the gathering of the Jews to migrate from Medina, or the gathering of the Muslims to expel them. From this, it becomes clear that this "Hashr" is not related to the Gathering (Hashr) on the Day of Resurrection.

Some have called this Surah "Surah Banu Nadir," as the major part of its verses concerns them.

Finally, this Surah is also one of the "Musabbihat" Surahs, which begin with the glorification of Allah, and coincidentally, it also ends with Divine glorification.

The Virtue of Reciting this Surah

Much has been said regarding the virtue of this Surah; among these, we read in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Hashr, there remains no Paradise, nor Fire, nor Throne, nor Pedestal, nor Veil, nor the seven heavens, nor the seven earths, nor insects, nor winds, nor birds, nor trees, nor animals, nor sun, nor moon, nor angels, except that they all send blessings upon him and seek forgiveness for him. And if he dies on that day or that night, he dies a martyr!" [1]

And in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Rahman and Al-Hashr in the evening, Allah appoints an angel for his house with a drawn sword to protect it until morning." [2]

Undoubtedly, all these are the effects of reflecting upon the content of the Surah that arise from its recitation and shine upon the life of man.

[1] and [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, pp. 255 and 256. Al-Qurtubi also cited the first Hadith at the beginning of this Surah.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ (1) هُوَ الَّذِي أَخْرَجَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ لِأَوَّلِ الْحَشْرِ ما ظَنَنْتُمْ أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا وَ ظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ مانِعَتُهُمْ حُصُونُهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ فَأَتاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَمْ يَحْتَسِبُوا وَ قَذَفَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الرُّعْبَ يُخْرِبُونَ بُيُوتَهُمْ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ وَ أَيْدِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ فَاعْتَبِرُوا يا أُولِي الْأَبْصارِ (2) وَ لَوْ لا أَنْ كَتَبَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْجَلاءَ لَعَذَّبَهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذابُ النَّارِ (3) ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَاقُّوا اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ وَ مَنْ يُشَاقِّ اللَّهَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ (4) ما قَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ لِينَةٍ أَوْ تَرَكْتُمُوها قائِمَةً عَلى أُصُولِها فَبِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ وَ لِيُخْزِيَ الْفاسِقِينَ (5)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators, Hadith scholars, and historians have mentioned a detailed occasion of revelation for these verses, the summary of which is as follows:

In the land of Medina, three groups of Jews lived: "Banu Nadir," "Banu Qurayza," and "Banu Qaynuqa." It is said that they were not originally from the Hijaz, but because they had read in their religious books that a Prophet would appear from the land of Medina, they migrated to this land and were awaiting this great appearance.

When the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله emigrated to Medina, He made a non-aggression pact with them, but whenever they found an opportunity, they did not hesitate to break this covenant.

Among these instances was that after the Battle of Uhud (the Battle of Uhud occurred in the third year of the Hijra), Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf came to Mecca with forty Jewish horsemen and went straight to the Quraysh, making a pact with them to fight unitedly against Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله. Then Abu Sufyan with forty Meccans and Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf with forty Jews entered Masjid al-Haram and solidified their covenants beside the Kaaba. This news reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله through revelation.

Another instance was when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله went one day with a few of His elders and companions to the tribe of Banu Nadir, who lived near Medina, seeking their help or a loan to pay the blood money (Diyah) for two people from the Banu 'Amir tribe who had been killed by 'Amr ibn Umayyah (a Muslim). Perhaps the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and His companions wanted to examine the situation of Banu Nadir closely under this cover, lest the Muslims be caught off guard.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was outside the Jewish fortress and spoke with Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf regarding this matter. At this moment, a seed of conspiracy was sown among the Jews, and they said to one another: "You will not find this man in such a favorable condition again. Now that He is sitting by your wall, let someone go to the roof and throw a massive stone upon Him to rid us of Him!" One of the Jews named 'Amr ibn Jihash volunteered and went to the roof. The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله became aware through revelation, stood up, and went to Medina without saying a word to His companions. They thought the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would return, but later learned He was in Medina. They also returned to Medina, and it was here that the Jews' breach of covenant became certain to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and He ordered the Muslims to prepare for war.

In some narrations, it is also mentioned that one of the poets of Banu Nadir began to satirize and malign the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which was itself another reason for their breach of covenant.

To strike a decisive blow beforehand, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered Muhammad ibn Maslamah, who was acquainted with Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, the Jewish leader, to kill him by whatever means possible, which he did after certain preparations.

The killing of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf created instability among the Jews. Following this, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the Muslims to move for war against this treaty-breaking people. When they heard the news, they took refuge in their fortified castles and strong strongholds and locked the gates firmly. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered that some palm trees near the fortresses be cut down or burned.

This action was perhaps taken to draw out the Jews—who had a strong attachment to their property—from the fortress so that a face-to-face battle could occur. It is also possible that these palms hindered the rapid maneuver of the Islamic army around the fortresses and had to be cleared.

In any case, this action caused the Jews to cry out, saying: "O Muhammad! You used to forbid such actions, so what is this agenda?" Verse five of this Surah (from the verses above) was revealed and answered them that this was a specific Divine command.

The siege lasted for several days, and to avoid bloodshed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله proposed that they leave the land of Medina and depart from there. They accepted, took some of their property, and left the rest. A group went toward Adhri'at in Syria, a small number toward Khaybar, and a group to Al-Hirah. Their remaining property, lands, orchards, and houses fell into the hands of the Muslims.

However, as much as they could, they destroyed their own houses upon departing.

This event occurred six months after the Battle of Uhud, or according to some, six months after the Battle of Badr. [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, Tafsir al-Qurtubi, and Nur al-Thaqalayn under the discussed verses (adapted and summarized).

Commentary

The End of the Banu Nadir Conspiracy in Medina

This Surah begins with the glorification and sanctification of Allah and the mention of His Might and Wisdom. It says: "Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the Almighty, the All-Wise" (سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ).

This is, in fact, an introduction to recounting the story of the Banu Nadir Jews—those who were caught in various deviations regarding the knowledge of Allah and His attributes, and those who relied on their own power and might and rose in conspiracy against the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

The universal glorification by the beings of the earth and heaven—including angels, humans, animals, plants, and inanimate objects—may be through the "language of speech" (qāl) or the "language of state" (hāl), for the wondrous system employed in the creation of every atom expresses Allah’s knowledge, power, greatness, and wisdom through its very existence.

On the other hand, according to a group of scholars, every being in its own world possesses a share of intellect, perception, and consciousness, even if we are unaware of it. For this reason, it glorifies Allah in its own language, even if our ears are not capable of hearing it. The entire world is a clamor of His glorification and praise, though we, the "uninitiated," are unaware of it. But those who have moved from the inanimate toward the Soul of souls, and for whom an eye has opened from the Unseen, are confidants with all the beings of the world and hear the speech of water and clay well, for this speech is perceptible to the "people of the heart"!

Whatever you wish is in clamor with His remembrance, A heart knows such meaning, for it is an ear. Not only is the nightingale a reciter of praise upon His rose, But every thorn is a tongue for His Oneness!

Further explanation regarding this statement is found under verse 44 of Surah Al-Isra (Vol. 12, pp. 133-140).

After stating this introduction, it addresses the story of the Banu Nadir Jews being driven out of Medina, saying: "He is the One who expelled those who disbelieved among the People of the Book from their homes at the first gathering" (هُوَ الَّذِي أَخْرَجَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ لِأَوَّلِ الْحَشْرِ).

"Hashr" originally means moving a population and taking them out of their station toward a battlefield or the like. What is meant here is the gathering and movement of the Muslims from Medina toward the Jewish fortresses, or the gathering of the Jews to fight the Muslims. Since this was the first gathering of its kind, it is named in the Quran as "the first gathering" (li-awwal al-hashr), which is itself a subtle allusion to future encounters with the Banu Nadir, the Jews of Khaybar, and their like.

Surprisingly, a group of commentators has offered possibilities for this verse that bear no relevance to its content, such as: it refers to the first gathering as opposed to the gathering on the Day of Resurrection from the graves toward the Place of Gathering. Even more surprising is that some have taken this verse as evidence that the Gathering of Resurrection will take place in the land of Syria, where the Jews were driven from Medina! It seems all these weak possibilities stem from the word "Hashr."

However, this term does not exclusively mean the Gathering of Resurrection; rather, it is applied to any kind of gathering, leaving a station, and appearing on a field, as we read in verse 17 of Surah Al-Naml: "And gathered for Solomon were his soldiers of jinn and men and birds" (وَ حُشِرَ لِسُلَيْمانَ جُنُودُهُ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَ الْإِنْسِ وَ الطَّيْرِ).

Similarly, regarding the gathering to witness the struggle of Moses with Pharaoh’s sorcerers, we read: "And that the people be gathered in the early forenoon" (وَ أَنْ يُحْشَرَ النَّاسُ ضُحًى) (Taha: 59).

Then it adds: "You did not think that they would leave, and they thought that their fortresses would protect them from Allah" (ما ظَنَنْتُمْ أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا وَ ظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ مانِعَتُهُمْ حُصُونُهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ).

They were so arrogant and self-satisfied that their reliance was on their strong fortresses and their outward power. This expression in the verse shows that the Banu Nadir Jews in Medina enjoyed extensive facilities and abundant equipment, such that neither they nor others believed they would be defeated so easily. But since Allah wanted to make it clear to everyone that nothing can resist His will, He drove them out of that land even without a battle occurring! Therefore, the verse continues: "But Allah came upon them from where they did not expect, and He cast terror into their hearts, so that they destroyed their houses with their own hands and the hands of the believers" (فَأَتاهُمُ اللَّهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَمْ يَحْتَسِبُوا وَ قَذَفَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الرُّعْبَ يُخْرِبُونَ بُيُوتَهُمْ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ وَ أَيْدِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

Yes, Allah made this invisible army—the army of terror, which He sent to aid the believers in many battles—prevail over their hearts, depriving them of any movement or confrontation. They had prepared themselves to face an external army, unaware that Allah would send an army from within them, placing them in such a strait that they themselves would cooperate with the enemy to destroy their own houses.

It is true that the killing of their leader, Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, before this event had cast terror into their hearts, but certainly, the meaning of the verse is not limited to that—as some commentators have imagined—rather, it was a type of Divine assistance that repeatedly aided the Muslims in Islamic battles.

Interestingly, the Muslims were destroying the fortresses from the outside to find a way in, and the Jews were destroying them from the inside so they would not fall intact into the hands of the Muslims. The result of this "cooperation" was the destruction of their fortifications! Regarding this verse, other interpretations have also been mentioned, including: the Jews destroyed the fortress walls from the inside to flee, and the Muslims from the outside to reach them (but this is unlikely).

It has also been said that this verse has a metaphorical meaning, like when we say someone destroyed his house and life with his own hands, meaning through ignorance and stubbornness he caused the ruin of his own life.

Or that the Jews' intention in destroying some houses was to block the entrances of the alleys inside the fortress to prevent the Muslims' advance and to ensure they could not dwell there in the future.

Or that they destroyed part of the houses inside the fortress so that if the battlefield moved inside, they would have enough space to fight.

Or that in the construction of some houses, there were expensive materials; they destroyed them to take what was portable. However, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all.

At the end of the verse, as a general conclusion, it says: "So take warning, O you who have eyes!" (فَاعْتَبِرُوا يا أُولِي الْأَبْصارِ).

"I'tabiru" (take warning) comes from the root "i'tibar," which originally comes from "ubur," meaning to pass from one thing to another. Tears are called "'abrah" because of the passage of tear drops from the eye. An "expression" (ibarah) is so called because it transfers meanings from one person to another. The term "interpretation of dreams" (ta'bir) is used because it transfers a person from the outward appearance of the dream to its inward meaning.

By this same token, events that give man a lesson are called "'ibrah" (warning/lesson), because they lead man to a series of general teachings and transfer him from one point to another.

The expression "Uli al-Absar" (those who have eyes) refers to those who see events well, scrutinize them with open eyes, and reach their depths.

(The word "basar" usually refers to the organ of sight, and "basirat" to internal perception and awareness). [1] In reality, "Uli al-Absar" are those who have the readiness to take lessons; thus, the Quran warns them to make the necessary use of this event.

There is no doubt that the purpose of "taking warning" is to compare similar events that are identical in the judgment of reason, such as comparing the state of other disbelievers and treaty-breakers to the Banu Nadir Jews. However, this sentence has no connection to "conjectural analogies" (qiyas) that some use in deriving religious rulings, and it is surprising that some Sunni jurists have used the above verse to prove this purpose, though others have critiqued it.

In short: the meaning of "warning" and "taking lesson" in the above verse is a logical and certain transition from one subject to another, not acting upon fancy and conjecture.

In any case, truly the fate of this Jewish people, with all that power, greatness, and glory, and with those abundant facilities and fortifications, was a lesson-giving fate. Even without taking up arms, they surrendered to the Muslim population—who outwardly never seemed their equal—destroyed their houses with their own hands, left their wealth for the needy Muslims, and scattered to different regions. This is while, according to historical reports, they had originally settled in the land of Medina for the purpose of witnessing the Promised Prophet of their books and being in the first rank of His companions! In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"The majority of Abu Dharr’s (may Allah have mercy on him) worship was reflection and taking warning." [1]

But unfortunately, many are those who must experience all painful events themselves and personally taste the bitterness of defeats, never taking warning from the situation of others.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says: "The fortunate one is he who is cautioned by [the example of] others!" [1]

The next verse adds: "And had it not been that Allah had decreed for them exile, He would have punished them in this world" (وَ لَوْ لا أَنْ كَتَبَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْجَلاءَ لَعَذَّبَهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا). Undoubtedly, exile and abandoning the major part of the wealth they had gathered over a lifetime was itself a painful punishment for them. Therefore, the meaning of the above sentence is that if this punishment had not been decreed for them, another punishment—which was death and captivity at the hands of the Muslims—would have descended upon them. Allah wanted them to be wanderers in the world, and perhaps this wandering was more painful for them, for whenever they remembered all those fortresses, magnificent houses, farms, and orchards now in the hands of others, while they themselves were deprived and wandering in other regions due to their breach of covenant and conspiracy against the Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, they would suffer great mental torment.

Yes, Allah wanted to afflict this arrogant, deceptive, and treaty-breaking group with such a painful fate.

But this was only their worldly punishment; thus, at the end of this verse, it adds: "And for them in the Hereafter is the punishment of the Fire" (وَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذابُ النَّارِ).

Such is the world and the hereafter of those who turn their backs on truth and justice and ride the mount of arrogance and selfishness.

Since the mention of this event, in addition to expressing the power of the Lord and the truthfulness of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, must be a warning for all those who commit acts similar to the Banu Nadir Jews—so that the matter is not limited to them—the next verse teaches the principle, adding: "That is because they opposed Allah and His Messenger" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ شَاقُّوا اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ).

"And whoever opposes Allah, then indeed Allah is severe in penalty" (وَ مَنْ يُشَاقِّ اللَّهَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ). [1] "Shaqqu" comes from the root "shiqaq," which originally means a crack or separation between two things. Since the enemy always stands on the opposite side and separates himself, his action is called "shiqaq."

This exact verse, with a very minor difference, appears in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 13, after the story of the Battle of Badr and the crushing of the polytheists, which indicates the universality of its content in every respect.

It is noteworthy that at the beginning of the verse, it mentions opposition to Allah and the Messenger, but at the end of the verse, it speaks only of opposition to Allah, indicating that opposition to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله is one and the same as opposition to Allah and is not separate from it.

The expression "severe in penalty" (shadid al-'iqab) in no way contradicts Allah being the "Most Merciful of the merciful," for where there is room for pardon and mercy, He is the Most Merciful, and where there is room for punishment and retribution, He is the "Severest of punishers," as stated in the supplication: "And I have become certain that You are the Most Merciful of the merciful in the context of pardon and mercy, and the Severest of punishers in the context of exemplary punishment and vengeance." [2]

In the last of the discussed verses, it addresses an objection that the Banu Nadir Jews—as we mentioned in the occasion of revelation—directed at the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. At that time, He ordered that some of the palm trees near the strong fortresses of the Jews be cut down (to provide sufficient space for battle, or so that the Jews would become distressed and come out of the fortresses, leading to a confrontation outside). They said: "O Muhammad! Were you not the one who used to forbid such things?" The verse was revealed and said: "Whatever you cut down of the 'linah' palm trees or left them standing on their trunks—it was by permission of Allah" (ما قَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ لِينَةٍ أَوْ تَرَكْتُمُوها قائِمَةً عَلى أُصُولِها فَبِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ). [1] And "that He might disgrace the defiantly disobedient" (وَ لِيُخْزِيَ الْفاسِقِينَ).

"Linah" comes from the root "lawn" and refers to a high-quality type of date palm. Some have interpreted it from the root "lin" (softness), referring to a type of palm tree that has soft branches close to the ground and soft, delicious fruit.

Sometimes "linah" is interpreted as "alwan" (various types) of palm trees or "noble palms," all of which essentially point to the same thing.

In any case, some have said: the act of cutting some of the Banu Nadir's palm trees was carried out by some Muslims, while others were opposed. Here, the above verse was revealed and settled the dispute between them. [2] Others have said: the verse refers to the actions of two companions; one of whom, when cutting the palms, cut the good trees to enrage the Jews (so they would descend from the fortress), and the other cut the low-value palms so that the valuable ones would remain for future use. Because of this, a disagreement arose between them; the verse was revealed and said both were by Divine permission. [1] However, the outward meaning of the verse is that the Muslims cut some "linah" (good quality) trees and left some of them, and this action caused the Jews' objection, and the Quran answered them to clarify that this act was not out of personal desire, but rather a Divine command had been issued in this regard to be carried out within a limited scope, so that no excessive damage would occur.

In any case, this ruling is an exception to the well-known Islamic law which states that when attacking an enemy, trees should not be cut, animals should not be killed, and crops should not be burned. This was only related to a case where it was necessary to draw the enemy out of the fortress or to prepare the battlefield and the like, and in every general law, there are often necessary specific exceptions, just as the general principle is not to eat carrion, but in cases of necessity and desperation, "eating carrion" is permitted.

The phrase "that He might disgrace the defiantly disobedient" (وَ لِيُخْزِيَ الْفاسِقِينَ) shows that at least one of the goals of this action was to humiliate the enemy and break their morale.

Key Points

1- The Invisible Armies of Allah!

While the people of the world rely on their outward and material forces to achieve victory, the reliance of God-worshippers is on their spiritual values and Divine assistance. We observed an example of this in the story of the defeat of the Banu Nadir and their expulsion from Medina in the verses above.

In these verses, we read that one of the effective factors of victory was the terror and horror that Allah cast into their hearts, to the point that they destroyed their houses with their own hands and agreed to forgo their wealth and leave that land.

A similar meaning has appeared several times in the verses of the Holy Quran, including in the story of another group of Jews called "Banu Qurayza," whom the Muslims faced in a severe confrontation after the Battle of the Trench (Ahzab). It says: "And He brought down those who supported them among the People of the Book from their fortresses and cast terror into their hearts [so that] a party you killed, and a party you took captive" (Al-Ahzab: 26).

The same meaning appears in the story of the Battle of Badr, which says: "I will cast terror into the hearts of those who disbelieved."

Part of this terror, which functions as an invisible Divine army, is natural, though part of it is mysterious, and its connections to ordinary means are not revealed to us. What is natural is that believers consider themselves victorious in any case—whether they are killed and become martyrs or they crush the enemy. Someone whose logic is this does not allow terror into himself; rather, such a wondrous human is terrifying to his enemy. Just as in today's world, we see large countries that, despite having the most advanced weapons and the largest armies, are terrified of a small group of true, struggling believers and constantly try not to engage with them.

In a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that He said:

"I have been aided by terror [in the hearts of the enemy] for the distance of a month's journey!" [1]

Meaning, not only are those who face Me on the battlefield terrified, but in places where the enemy is a month's journey away from Me, terror and anxiety prevail! Regarding the soldiers of Imam al-Mahdi (may Allah hasten his reappearance), we also read that three armies aid him: "The angels, the believers, and terror!" [2] In reality, they try not to be struck from the outside, but Allah disintegrates them from within, and we know that an internal blow is more agonizing and irreparable, for if all the weapons and armies of the world are at someone's disposal but he lacks the spirit to fight, he is doomed to defeat and destruction.

2- Today's Jewish Conspiracies

The history of Islam from its very beginning was intertwined with their conspiracies, and in many painful events, we see them either on the stage or behind the scenes. Surprisingly, they came to the land of Hijaz out of love for the Promised Prophet, but after this great appearance, they became fierce enemies. Even today, in most anti-Islamic conspiracies, we see the Jews on the stage or behind the scenes, and this is a lesson for those who have insight! As the history of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله shows, the only way to repel them is through decisive confrontation, especially the "Zionists" in whose logic there is no moderation, justice, or fairness. Their language is force, and one cannot speak to them except with force and power. Yet, despite this, they fear true believers more than anything else. If Muslims today possessed the same faith, steadfastness, and decisiveness as the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, terror and horror would prevail over the hearts of these bloodthirsty enemies, and they could be driven out of the occupied lands with this very Divine army. This is a lesson that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله gave us fourteen centuries ago.

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ مِنْهُمْ فَمَآ أَوْجَفْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ خَيْلٍ وَلَا رِكَابٍ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسَلِّطُ رُسُلَهُۥ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٦)
The spoils that Allah gave to His Apostle from them, you did not spur any horse for its sake, nor any riding camel, but Allah makes His apostles prevail over whomever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (6)
مَّآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْقُرَىٰ فَلِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ كَىْ لَا يَكُونَ دُولَةًۢ بَيْنَ ٱلْأَغْنِيَآءِ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمُ ٱلرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَمَا نَهَىٰكُمْ عَنْهُ فَٱنتَهُوا۟ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٧)
The spoils that Allah gave to His Apostle from the people of the townships, are for Allah and the Apostle, the relatives and the orphans, the needy and the traveller, so that they do not circulate among the rich among you. Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and refrain from whatever he forbids you, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is severe in retribution. (7)

Occasion of Revelation

Since these verses complement the previous ones recounting the story of the defeat of the Jewish tribe of Banu al-Nadir, their occasion of revelation is a continuation of the same events.

To explain: after the Banu al-Nadir Jews were expelled from Medina, their gardens, agricultural lands, houses, and a portion of their property remained in Medina. A group of Muslim leaders came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and, according to what they remembered from the traditions of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), they said: "Take the choice parts of this booty and one-fourth of it for yourself, and leave the rest to us so we may divide it among ourselves!" The above verses were revealed and explicitly stated: since no war was fought for this booty and the Muslims underwent no hardship, all of it belongs to the Messenger of Allah (the head of the Islamic government), and He shall distribute it as He deems fit. As we shall see later, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله distributed these properties among the Emigrants (Muhajirin), whose hands were empty of worldly wealth in the land of Medina, and a small number of the Helpers (Ansar) who were in dire need. [^1]

Commentary

The Ruling on Spoils Obtained Without War

These verses, as mentioned, explain the ruling regarding the spoils of Banu al-Nadir, and at the same time, they clarify a general law concerning all spoils that come to the Islamic society without trouble, effort, or suffering, which is referred to in Islamic jurisprudence as "Fay’."

It says: "And what Allah restored [as Fay’] to His Messenger from them—you did not spur for it any horse or camel (وَ ما أَفاءَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ رَسُولِهِ مِنْهُمْ فَما أَوْجافْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ خَيْلٍ وَ لا رِكابٍ)." [^1] The word "Afa’a" is derived from the root "Fay’," which originally means "return or restoration." The reason this category of spoils is called "Fay’" (rhyming with Shay’) is perhaps because Allah originally created all the blessings of this world for the believers, and above all for His noble Prophet, who is the most honorable of creation and the essence of existence. Non-believers and sinners are, in reality, usurpers of these properties (even if they are considered owners according to religious or customary laws). When these properties return to their true owners, they deserve the title "Fay’."

"Awjaftum" is from the root "Ijfaf," meaning to drive fast, which usually happens in wars.

"Khayl" means horses (it is a collective noun that has no singular form of its own). [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the discussed verses, and other commentaries.

[^1]: The "Ma" in "Ma afa’a Allah" is a relative pronoun (mawsulah) and serves as the subject (mubtada), and the "Ma" in "fa-ma awjaftum 'alayhi" is a negation (nafiyah), and the entire sentence is the predicate (khabar). It is also possible that the first "Ma" is conditional and the second, along with the following phrase, is the response to the condition (the appearance of "fa" before the predicate when it resembles a condition is permissible).

[^2]: Raghib in Al-Mufradat says "Khayl" is originally from the root "Khayal," meaning thought or mental imagery, and "Khuyala’" means arrogance and self-superiority because it stems from a kind of imagined virtue. Since a person often feels a sense of pride and arrogance when riding a horse, the word "Khayl" was applied to horses. Notably, "Khayl" refers to both the horses and the riders.

"Rikab" from the root "Rukub" usually refers to riding camels.

The objective of the entire phrase is that in all cases where no war occurs to obtain booty, the spoils will not be divided among the warriors; rather, they are placed entirely at the disposal of the leader of the Muslims. He then spends them according to his discretion on the expenditures mentioned in the following verse.

Then it adds: It is not the case that victories are always the result of your battles, "but Allah gives His messengers authority over whom He wills, and Allah is over all things competent" (وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يُسَلِّطُ رُسُلَهُ عَلى‌ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

Yes, the victory over a stubborn and powerful enemy like the Banu al-Nadir Jews was achieved through Allah's unseen aid, so that you may know Allah is capable of all things. He can humble a powerful people in the blink of an eye, give a small group authority over them, and transfer all resources from the first group to the second.

It is here that Muslims can learn a lesson in the knowledge of Allah (Ma'rifat Allah), see the signs of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله truthfulness, and learn the program of sincerity and reliance on the Pure Essence of God throughout their path.

A question arises here: the spoils of the Banu al-Nadir Jews did not come into the possession of the Muslims without war; rather, they launched a military expedition and surrounded the Jewish fortresses in a siege, and it is even said that a limited armed clash occurred.

In response, we say: the fortresses of Banu al-Nadir, as stated, were not far from Medina (some commentators mention the distance as two miles, less than 4 kilometers), and the Muslims went to the fortresses on foot; therefore, they did not endure significant hardship.

As for the occurrence of an armed clash, it is not historically proven, and the siege did not last long. Therefore, it can be said that in reality, nothing occurred that could be called a battle, and no blood was shed on the ground.

The next verse clearly explains the expenditure of "Fay’" mentioned in the previous verse and states as a general rule: "Whatever Allah restored to His Messenger from the people of the towns—it is for Allah and for the Messenger and for [his] near relatives and orphans and the needy and the [stranded] traveler" (ما أَفاءَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ رَسُولِهِ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرى‌ فَلِلَّهِ وَ لِلرَّسُولِ وَ لِذِي الْقُرْبى‌ وَ الْيَتامى‌ وَ الْمَساكِينِ وَ ابْنِ السَّبِيلِ‌).

This means it is not like the spoils of armed wars, where only one-fifth is placed at the disposal of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and other needy people, while four-fifths belong to the warriors.

Furthermore, if the previous verse stated that all of it belongs to the Messenger of Allah, it does not mean He spends all of it on personal expenses. Rather, because He is the head of the Islamic government, and especially the defender and protector of the rights of the needy, He spends the major portion on them.

In this verse, six categories of expenditure for "Fay’" are mentioned:

  1. The share of Allah: Obviously, Allah is the owner of everything and is in no need of anything. This is a type of honorific attribution so that the other groups mentioned afterward do not feel any sense of inferiority and consider their share alongside Allah's share, ensuring their dignity is not diminished in public opinion.

  2. The share of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: Which naturally covers His personal needs, then the requirements of His office and the expectations people have of Him.

  3. The share of the Near Relatives (Dhu al-Qurba): Without doubt, this refers here to the relatives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Banu Hashim, who are prohibited from receiving Zakat, which is part of the general public funds of the Muslims. [^1] Fundamentally, it makes no sense for it to mean the relatives of the general public, as in that case, it would include all Muslims without exception, since all people are relatives of one another.

There is discussion among commentators as to whether need and poverty are conditions for the "Near Relatives," though based on the evidence at the end of this verse and the following verse, the condition of need seems more correct.

4, 5, and 6. The shares of the orphans, the needy, and the stranded travelers: There is discussion among commentators whether these three groups must only be from the Banu Hashim or if they include all orphans, the poor, and travelers.

The majority of Sunni jurists and their commentators believe this is general, while the narrations reached through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام vary on this matter. From some, it is understood that these three shares are also specific to the orphans, needy, and travelers of the Banu Hashim, while in other narrations, it is explicitly stated that this ruling is general. It is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said:

"My father used to say: The share of the Messenger of Allah and the share of the Near Relatives belong to us, and we are partners with the people in what remains [of the other shares]." [^1]

Verses 8 and 9 of this same Surah, which provide an explanation for this verse, also testify that this share is not exclusive to the Banu Hashim, as they speak of the general poor among the Emigrants and the Helpers.

Furthermore, commentators have narrated that after the event of Banu al-Nadir, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله divided the property left by them among the "Emigrants," who were generally living in difficult conditions in Medina, and three individuals from the "Helpers" who were in dire need. This is evidence for the generality of the verse's meaning, and if some narrations are inconsistent with it, the apparent meaning of the Quran must be preferred. [^2]

Then, addressing the philosophy of this calculated distribution, it adds: "This is so that it will not be a perpetual distribution among the rich from among you" (كَيْ لا يَكُونَ دُولَةً بَيْنَ الْأَغْنِياءِ مِنْكُمْ). [^3]

A group of commentators has mentioned a specific occasion of revelation for this sentence, which was briefly alluded to earlier: after the Banu al-Nadir incident, a group of Muslim leaders came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "Take your choice portion and one-fourth of these spoils, and leave the rest to us to divide among ourselves, just as it was in the time of Ignorance before Islam!" The above verse was revealed and warned them: this wealth must not circulate only among the rich!

This verse expresses a fundamental principle in Islamic economics: that the direction of Islamic economics is such that while respecting "private property," it has organized the system so that wealth and riches do not become concentrated in the hands of a limited group, circulating perpetually among them.

Of course, this does not mean we should arbitrarily enact laws to take wealth from one group and give it to another. Rather, it means that if Islamic regulations regarding the acquisition of wealth, as well as taxes such as Khums, Zakat, Kharaj, and others, and the rulings of the Public Treasury (Bayt al-Mal) and Anfal are correctly implemented, they will automatically produce such a result. While respecting individual efforts, the collective interests will be secured, and the polarization of society (a wealthy minority and a poor majority) will be prevented.

At the end of the verse, it says: "And whatever the Messenger has given you—take it; and what he has forbidden you—refrain from. And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is severe in penalty" (وَ ما آتاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَ ما نَهاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانْتَهُوا وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ‌).

Although this sentence was revealed regarding the spoils of Banu al-Nadir, its content is a general ruling in all fields and programs of Muslim life. It is a clear document for the authority of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله Sunnah.

According to this principle, all Muslims are duty-bound to listen with their souls to the commands and prohibitions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and obey them, whether in matters related to the Islamic government, economic issues, worship, or otherwise. Especially since the end of the verse threatens those who oppose with severe punishment.

Key Points

1. The Expenditure of "Fay’" (Spoils Without War)

The properties placed under the title of "Fay’" at the disposal of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله as the leader of the Islamic government were vast. They included all properties that came into the possession of Muslims through means other than war. These properties could play a significant role in balancing wealth in the Islamic environment because, contrary to the tradition of Ignorance, they were never divided among the wealthy of the clans and tribes. Instead, they were directly at the disposal of the leader of the Muslims, who distributed them based on priorities.

As we have stated in the discussion of Anfal, "Fay’" is a part of Anfal. Another part consists of all properties that have no specific owner, the details of which are given in Islamic jurisprudence and amount to twelve subjects. In this way, a larger volume of divine blessings comes under the control of the Islamic government and subsequently the needy through this channel. [^1]

From what we have said, it becomes clear that there is no contradiction between the first and second verses mentioned above, even though the first verse apparently places "Fay’" at the disposal of the Prophet personally and the second verse mentions six categories of expenditure. This is because these six categories list the priorities that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله must observe regarding the wealth at His disposal. In other words, the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله does not want all this wealth for His personal self, but rather spends it wherever necessary in His capacity as the leader and head of the Islamic government.

It is also noteworthy that this right, after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, passes to the Infallible Imams عليهم السلام and after them to their deputies, namely the Mujtahids who meet all the necessary conditions. This is because Islamic rulings are not subject to suspension, and the Islamic government is one of the most important matters concerning Muslims, and part of the foundations of this government is built on economic issues, of which these are original Islamic economic matters.

2. Answer to a Question

A question may be raised here: how can Allah command all people without exception to accept unconditionally whatever the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله says?!

However, considering that we believe the Prophet to be infallible (Ma'sum), and that this right belongs only to Him and His infallible successors, the answer to the question becomes clear.

Interestingly, many narrations point out that if Allah gave such authority to His Prophet, it is because He tested Him thoroughly and He possesses a "sublime character" (Khuluq 'Azim) and extraordinary ethics, which is why such a right was delegated to Him. [^1]

3. The Tragic Story of Fadak:

"Fadak" was one of the prosperous villages around Medina, about 140 kilometers away near Khaybar. In the seventh year of the Hijra, when the fortresses of Khaybar fell one after another before the warriors of Islam and the central power of the Jews was broken, the inhabitants of Fadak sought peace and submission to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. They ceded half of their land and gardens to Him and kept the other half for themselves, while also undertaking the farming of the Prophet's share in exchange for a portion of the yield.

According to the verse of "Fay’" in this Surah, this land belonged specifically to the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and He could use it for Himself or for the other expenditures mentioned in Verse 7 of this Surah. Therefore, the Prophet gifted it to His daughter Fatima عليها سلام اللَّه. This is a fact stated by many Shia and Sunni historians and commentators. For instance, in the commentary Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that when the verse "And give the relative his right" (30:38) was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله granted Fadak to Fatima (Aqta'a Rasul Allah Fatima Fadaka). [^2]

[^1]: The twelve subjects of Anfal are as follows: 1. Lands whose inhabitants have abandoned them (like the lands of the Banu al-Nadir Jews). 2. Lands whose owners voluntarily ceded them to the leader of the Muslims (like Fadak). 3. Dead lands (Mawat). 4. Sea coasts. 5. Mountain peaks. 6. Valleys. 7. Thickets and forests. 8. Choice properties of kings captured in war. 9. What the leader of the Muslims selects for himself from the spoils. 10. Spoils obtained through wars fought without the permission of the leader of the Muslims. 11. Mines. 12. The inheritance of one who has no heir. (Of course, there is discussion among jurists regarding some of the above, but the overwhelming majority accept these cases. Refer to books of jurisprudence for more detail).

[^1]: Narrations discussing this are numerous; refer to Vol. 5 of Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, pp. 279 to 283.

[^2]: Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 4, p. 177.

In the book Kanz al-Ummal, which is in the margins of Musnad Ahmad, it is narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri regarding the issue of maintaining family ties that when the above verse was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله called Fatima عليه السلام and said: "O Fatima! Fadak is yours" (Ya Fatima laka Fadak). [^1] Hakim al-Nishapuri also recorded this in his history. [^2] Ibn Abi al-Hadid also mentioned the story of Fadak in detail in the Commentary on Nahj al-Balagha, [^3] as do numerous other books.

However, after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, those who saw the existence of this economic power in the hands of Ali's عليه السلام wife as an obstacle to their political power and decided to isolate Ali's عليه السلام companions in every respect, confiscated it under the pretext of the fabricated hadith: "We, the assembly of Prophets, do not leave inheritance." Even though Fatima عليه السلام was officially in possession of it, and one does not demand witnesses from the one in possession (Dhu al-Yad), they demanded witnesses from her. The Lady عليه السلام also produced witnesses that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had personally gifted Fadak to her, but they paid no heed. In later periods, whenever a caliph wanted to show an inclination toward the Ahl al-Bayt, he would return Fadak to them, but it would not be long before another would confiscate it again! This act was repeated many times during the Umayyad and Abbasid eras.

The story of Fadak and the various events related to it in early Islam and subsequent periods are among the most painful, tragic, and yet instructive chapters of Islamic history, which must be studied independently and precisely to unveil various events of Islamic history.

Notably, the Sunni traditionist Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj al-Nishapuri, in his famous book Sahih Muslim, recorded the story of Fatima's عليه السلام demand for Fadak from the first caliph in detail. He narrates from Aisha that after the caliph's refusal to hand over Fadak, Fatima عليه السلام was upset with him and did not speak a single word to him until her passing (Sahih Muslim, Vol. 3, p. 1380, Hadith 52 from the Book of Jihad).

[^1]: Kanz al-Ummal, Vol. 2, p. 158.

[^2]: Refer to the book Fadak, p. 49.

[^3]: Sharh Ibn Abi al-Hadid, Vol. 16, p. 209 onwards.

Ayahs

لِلْفُقَرَآءِ ٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ وَأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنًا وَيَنصُرُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقُونَ (٨)
[They are also] for the poor Emigrants who have been expelled from their homes and [wrested of] their possessions, who seek grace from Allah and [His] pleasure and help Allah and His Apostle. It is they who are the truthful. (8)
وَٱلَّذِينَ تَبَوَّءُو ٱلدَّارَ وَٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ يُحِبُّونَ مَنْ هَاجَرَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَا يَجِدُونَ فِى صُدُورِهِمْ حَاجَةً مِّمَّآ أُوتُوا۟ وَيُؤْثِرُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَوْ كَانَ بِهِمْ خَصَاصَةٌ ۚ وَمَن يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٩)
[They are as well] for those who were settled in the land and [abided] in faith before them, who love those who migrate toward them, and do not find in their breasts any privation for that which is given to them, but prefer [the Immigrants] to themselves, though poverty be their own lot. And those who are saved from their own greed—it is they who are the felicitous. (9)
وَٱلَّذِينَ جَآءُو مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَلِإِخْوَٰنِنَا ٱلَّذِينَ سَبَقُونَا بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ وَلَا تَجْعَلْ فِى قُلُوبِنَا غِلًّا لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٠)
And [also for] those who came in after them, who say, ‘Our Lord, forgive us and our brethren who were our forerunners in the faith, and do not put any rancour in our hearts toward the faithful. Our Lord, You are indeed most kind and merciful.’ (10)

Commentary

Three Groups—The Migrants, the Helpers, and the Followers—and the Distinguished Qualities of Each

These verses continue the discussion from the previous verses regarding the six categories of "Fay’" (properties and spoils obtained by Muslims without war). In reality, they serve as an explanation for the orphans, the needy, and the wayfarers, and most notably, an explanation of the "wayfarer" (ibn al-sabil). This is because the largest number of Migrant (Muhajir) Muslims at that time consisted of those who were not needy in their own homelands, but had become destitute due to their migration.

It says: "This property is for the poor Migrants who were expelled from their homes and their properties" (لِلْفُقَراءِ الْمُهاجِرِينَ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ وَ أَمْوالِهِمْ) [1] "They seek bounty from Allah and His pleasure, and they aid Allah and His Messenger; it is they who are the truthful" (يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رِضْواناً وَ يَنْصُرُونَ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصَّادِقُونَ).

[^1]: "Lil-fuqara" (for the poor) is an appositive (badal) and serves as an explanation for "ibn al-sabil" (the wayfarer).

Here, three important characteristics are mentioned for the early Migrants, which can be summarized as "Sincerity," "Jihad," and "Truthfulness."

First, it raises the issue of "seeking Allah’s bounty and His pleasure," which reflects the reality that their migration was not for worldly gain or personal desire, but to attain the satisfaction of the Lord and His reward.

Therefore, "bounty" (fadl) here means "reward," and "pleasure" (ridwan) is the satisfaction of the Lord, which is a higher stage than merely desiring reward. This is similar to several other verses in the Quran, including verse 29 of Surah Al-Fath, where the companions of the Messenger of Allah are described with this phrase: تراهُمْ رُكَّعاً سُجَّداً يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رِضْواناً: "You see them bowing and prostrating, seeking bounty from Allah and His pleasure."

The use of the term "bounty" (fadl) might even point to the fact that they consider their deeds too insignificant to deserve a reward; rather, they view the reward as a divine favor and gift. Some commentators have interpreted "bounty" here to mean worldly sustenance, as it appears with this meaning in some other verses, but considering that the context is describing the sincerity of the Migrants, this meaning is not suitable; rather, divine reward is more appropriate.

Of course, it is not unlikely that "bounty" refers to the physical blessings of Paradise, while "pleasure" refers to spiritual and intellectual blessings; regardless, both pertain to the Hereafter, not this world.

Secondly, they are constantly in the service of the religion of truth and aiding His Messenger, never ceasing from Jihad in this path for even a moment. (Note that the phrase "yansurun" is in the present continuous tense, indicating persistence). Thus, they are not people of mere words and claims; rather, they have proven their faith through continuous struggle.

In the third stage, it describes them with "truthfulness" (sidq). Given the breadth of this term's concept, it reflects their honesty in everything: they are truthful in their claim of faith, truthful in their claim of love for the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and truthful in their support for the religion of truth.

It goes without saying that these descriptions applied to the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله at the time these verses were revealed. However, we know that among them were individuals who later changed their path and deprived themselves of the great honors mentioned in this verse—such as those who ignited the fires of the Battle of Jamal in Basra and Siffin in Sham, rising against the Caliph of the Messenger of Allah whose obedience was mandatory by the consensus of Muslims, and spilling the blood of thousands of Muslims, and others like them.

In the next verse, it addresses another use for these properties and, in doing so, provides a very beautiful and eloquent description of the group of Helpers (Ansar), completing the discussion about the Migrants from the previous verse. It says: "And those who settled in the Abode (the land of Medina) and in faith before them" (وَ الَّذِينَ تَبَوَّؤُا الدَّارَ وَ الْإِيمانَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

It is noteworthy that "tabawwa’u" comes from the root "bawa’," which originally means the equality of the parts of a place. In other words, to level and arrange a place is called "bawa’." This expression is a subtle metaphor for the fact that the Ansar of Medina prepared the grounds for migration before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Migrants entered the city. As history records, they came twice to Aqaba (a mountain pass near Mecca), secretly pledged allegiance to the Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and returned to Medina as preachers. They even brought one of the Muslims of Mecca named Mus'ab ibn Umayr with them to Medina as a preacher to prepare public opinion for the Prophet's migration.

Therefore, they not only prepared physical houses to welcome the Migrants but also prepared the "house" of their hearts and souls, and the environment of their city, as much as they could.

The phrase "before them" (min qablihim) shows that all of this occurred before the migration of the Muslims from Mecca, and this is the key point.

According to this interpretation, the Ansar of Medina were also among those entitled to these properties. This does not contradict what is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—that he gave from the properties of Bani Nadir to only two or three individuals from the Ansar—because it is possible that there were no needy or poor individuals among the Ansar except those few, whereas the condition for receiving these properties was poverty and need. In contrast, the Migrants, even if they did not fit the definition of "poor," were considered "wayfarers" (ibn al-sabil). [1]

Then, it proceeds to three other descriptions that reflect the entire spirit of the Ansar, stating: "They love those who migrated to them" (يُحِبُّونَ مَنْ هاجَرَ إِلَيْهِمْ).

In this regard, there is no difference among Muslims in their eyes; rather, what matters to them is the issue of faith and migration, and this love is considered a continuous characteristic of theirs.

"Secondly, they find no need in their hearts for what the Migrants have been given" (وَ لا يَجِدُونَ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ حاجَةً مِمَّا أُوتُوا).

They neither have an eye on the spoils given to the Migrants, nor do they feel envy toward them, nor do they even feel a personal need in their hearts for what was granted to the Migrants. Such thoughts do not even cross their minds, which demonstrates the ultimate high-mindedness and nobility of the Ansar.

In the third stage, it adds: "And they give them preference over themselves, even though they are in desperate poverty" (وَ يُؤْثِرُونَ عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَ لَوْ كانَ بِهِمْ خَصاصَةٌ). [2] Thus, "love," "high-mindedness," and "self-sacrifice" (ithar) are their three proud characteristics.

Commentators have narrated several stories regarding the occasion of revelation (shan al-nuzul) for this verse. Ibn Abbas says: On the day of victory over the Jews of Bani Nadir, the Holy Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said to the Ansar: "If you wish, you may divide your properties and houses with the Migrants and share in these spoils with them; or if you wish, your properties and houses shall remain yours, and nothing of these spoils shall be given to you."

The Ansar replied: "We will divide our properties and houses with them, and we have no eye on the spoils, and we give the Migrants preference over ourselves." Then the above verse was revealed, praising their sublime spirit. [3]

In another hadith, we read: A man came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "I am hungry." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered food to be brought from his house, but there was no food in his home. He asked: "Who will host this man tonight?" A man from the Ansar volunteered and took him to his home, but he had nothing except a small amount of food for his children. He instructed that the food be brought for the guest and extinguished the lamp. He told his wife: "Do whatever you can to make the children sleep." Then the husband and wife sat at the table, and without putting any food in their mouths, they moved their jaws. The guest thought they were eating with him; he ate enough and was satisfied, while they slept hungry that night. In the morning, they came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله looked at them and smiled, and (without them saying a word) he recited the above verse and praised their self-sacrifice.

In narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, we read: The host was Ali عليه السلام and the children were his sons, and the one who put the children to sleep hungry was the Lady of Islam, Fatima al-Zahra عليها السلام. [4]

It should be noted that the first story may be the occasion of revelation, while the second is a form of application (tatbiq) by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who recited the verse regarding this act of self-sacrificing hospitality. Therefore, the revelation of the verses concerning the Ansar does not contradict Ali عليه السلام being the host.

[^1]: However, according to another interpretation, "وَ الَّذِينَ تَبَوَّؤُا الدَّارَ" is the subject (mubtada) and "yuhibbun" is its predicate (khabar), forming an independent sentence unrelated to the previous sentence about the distribution of "Fay’." But clearly, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

[^3] and [^4]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 260.

Some have also written that this verse was revealed concerning the warriors of Uhud, seven of whom were severely thirsty and wounded. Someone brought enough water for one person to drink; he went to each one, but each referred him to the next, giving preference to the other. Ultimately, they all passed away thirsty, and Allah praised this self-sacrifice of theirs. [1] However, it is clear that this verse was revealed in the story of Bani Nadir, but due to the generality of its concept, it is applicable to similar cases.

At the end of the verse, to further emphasize these noble qualities and state their result, it adds: "And whoever is protected from the stinginess and greed of his own soul—it is they who are the successful" (وَ مَنْ يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ).

"Shuhh," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means stinginess combined with greed that has become a habit.

"Yuqa" comes from the root "wiqayah" (protection). Although it is in the passive voice, it is evident that the agent here is Allah; meaning, whoever Allah protects from this blameworthy trait is successful.

In a hadith, we read that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said to one of his companions: "Do you know who the shahiih is?" He replied: "It is the miser (bakheel)." The Imam said: "Shuhh is more severe than bukhl (stinginess). The miser is stingy with what is in his own hand, but the shahiih is stingy both with what is in people's hands and what is in his own, to the point that he sees nothing in people's hands without wishing it were his, whether through lawful or unlawful means, and he is never content with what Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, has provided for him!" [2]

In another hadith, we read: "Greed (shuhh) and faith never gather in the heart of a Muslim man! And the dust of the path of Allah and the smoke of Hell never gather inside a Muslim man!" [3]

In short: it is clearly understood from the above verse that abandoning stinginess and greed leads a person to salvation, while being tainted by this blameworthy trait destroys the palace of human happiness.

In the final verse under discussion, it speaks of a third group of Muslims who, inspired by the Holy Quran, have become known among us as the "Followers" (Tabi'in). They constitute the third great group of Muslims after the Migrants and the Helpers mentioned in the previous verses.

It says: "And those who came after them say: 'Our Lord! Forgive us and our brothers who preceded us in faith, and do not put in our hearts any resentment or malice toward those who have believed. Our Lord! Indeed, You are Kind and Merciful'" (وَ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لَنا وَ لِإِخْوانِنَا الَّذِينَ سَبَقُونا بِالْإِيمانِ وَ لا تَجْعَلْ فِي قُلُوبِنا غِلًّا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ).

[^1]: Same source. ^2: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 291, Hadith 64. [^3]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 262.

Although some commentators have limited the concept of this phrase to those who joined the Muslims after the victory of Islam and the conquest of Mecca, there is no evidence for this limitation. Rather, it includes all Muslims until the Day of Resurrection. Even if the verse were focused on that specific group, it is universal in terms of its criteria and result. Thus, these three verses encompass all the Muslims of the world, categorized under the three titles of "Migrants," "Helpers," and "Followers."

The phrase "And those who came..." (وَ الَّذِينَ جاؤُ) is apparently conjoined to "the poor Migrants" (لِلْفُقَراءِ الْمُهاجِرِينَ) and reflects the reality that the "Fay’" properties are not exclusive to the needy among the Migrants and Helpers, but include other needy Muslims throughout history.

The possibility has also been raised that it is an independent sentence (where "those who came" is the subject and "they say" is the predicate), but the first interpretation seems more appropriate given its harmony with the preceding verses.

It is noteworthy that here too, three qualities are mentioned for the Followers:

First, they are concerned with their own self-improvement and seeking forgiveness and repentance before Allah.

Second, they look upon those who preceded them in faith as elder brothers who are respected in every way, and they ask for forgiveness for them from Allah.

Third, they strive to purge all malice, enmity, and envy from their hearts, seeking help from the Kind and Merciful Lord in this path. Thus, "self-building," "respect for the pioneers in faith," and "avoiding malice and envy" are among their characteristics.

"Ghill," as we have said before, originally means the secret penetration of something; hence, water flowing among trees is called "ghalal." Since envy, enmity, and hostility penetrate the human heart in a mysterious way, they are called "ghill." Therefore, "ghill" does not only mean "envy" but has a broad meaning that includes many hidden and ugly moral traits.

The use of the term "ikhwan" (brothers) and the seeking of help from the Kind and Merciful Lord at the end of the verse all indicate the spirit of love, purity, and brotherhood that must govern the entire Islamic society. No one should want any good only for themselves; rather, efforts and requests should all be collective and for the collective. All malice, enmity, hostility, stinginess, greed, and envy should be washed away from the chests—and this is a true Islamic society.

Key Point: The Companions in the Balance of the Quran and History

Here, some commentators, without paying attention to the qualities mentioned for each of the "Migrants," "Helpers," and "Followers" in the above verses, still insist on considering all "Companions" (Sahaba) as pure and infallible without exception. They overlook the wrongdoings that occasionally occurred from some of them during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or after him, and they blindly consider anyone categorized as a Migrant, Helper, or Follower as respected and holy.

However, the above verses provide a crushing response to these individuals and precisely define the criteria for the true "Migrants," "Helpers," and "Followers."

For the "Migrants," it lists sincerity, Jihad, and truthfulness.

For the "Helpers," it mentions love for the Migrants, self-sacrifice, and avoidance of all stinginess and greed.

For the "Followers," it describes self-building, respect for the pioneers in faith, and avoidance of all malice and envy.

Given this, how can we respect those who, for example, participated in the Battle of Jamal and drew swords against their Imam, neither observing Islamic brotherhood, nor purging their chests of malice, envy, and stinginess, nor respecting the precedence in faith of Ali عليه السلام? How can we consider any criticism of them a sin and blindly submit to the words of this person or that?

Therefore, while we respect the pioneers on the path of faith, we precisely examine the record of their actions, both during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and during the severe storms that broke out in the Islamic society after him. Based on the criteria we have derived from these very verses of the Quran, we judge and pass verdict on them. We strengthen our bond with those who remained faithful to their covenant and promise, and we distance ourselves from those who broke their bond during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or after him. This is a sound logic, consistent with the decree of the Quran and reason.

Ayahs

۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ نَافَقُوا۟ يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ وَلَا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَدًا أَبَدًا وَإِن قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنصُرَنَّكُمْ وَٱللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (١١)
Have you not regarded the hypocrites who say to their brethren, the faithless from among the People of the Book, ‘If you are expelled, we will surely go out with you, and we will never obey anyone against you, and if you are fought against we will surely help you,’ and Allah bears witness that they are indeed liars. (11)
لَئِنْ أُخْرِجُوا۟ لَا يَخْرُجُونَ مَعَهُمْ وَلَئِن قُوتِلُوا۟ لَا يَنصُرُونَهُمْ وَلَئِن نَّصَرُوهُمْ لَيُوَلُّنَّ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ ثُمَّ لَا يُنصَرُونَ (١٢)
Surely, if they were expelled they will not go out with them, and if they were fought against they will not help them, and [even if] they were to help them they will turn their backs [to flee] and eventually they will not be helped. (12)
لَأَنتُمْ أَشَدُّ رَهْبَةً فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ (١٣)
Indeed they have a greater awe of you in their hearts than of Allah. That is because they are a lot who do not understand. (13)
لَا يُقَـٰتِلُونَكُمْ جَمِيعًا إِلَّا فِى قُرًى مُّحَصَّنَةٍ أَوْ مِن وَرَآءِ جُدُرٍۭ ۚ بَأْسُهُم بَيْنَهُمْ شَدِيدٌ ۚ تَحْسَبُهُمْ جَمِيعًا وَقُلُوبُهُمْ شَتَّىٰ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْقِلُونَ (١٤)
They will not fight against you together except in fortified townships or from behind walls. Their fierceness is great only within themselves. You suppose them to be united, but their hearts are divided. That is because they are a lot who do not exercise their reason, (14)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated an occasion of revelation for the above verses, the summary of which is as follows:

A group of the hypocrites of Medina, such as "Abdullah ibn Ubayy" and his companions, secretly sent someone to the Jews of "Banu Nadir" and said: "Stand firm in your positions, do not leave your homes, and fortify your strongholds. We have two thousand helpers from our own people and others, and we are with you until the last breath. The tribe of Banu Qurayza and your other allies from the Ghatafan tribe will also support you."

This matter encouraged the Jews of "Banu Nadir" to oppose the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. However, at this time, one of the elders of Banu Nadir named "Sallam" said to "Huyayy ibn Akhtab," who was the leader of Banu Nadir's affairs: "Do not pay attention to the words of Abdullah ibn Ubayy; he wants to encourage you to war against Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله while he sits at home and leaves you to face the consequences." Huyayy replied: "We know nothing but enmity toward Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and fighting him." Sallam responded to him: "By Allah, I see that eventually we will be expelled from this land, our wealth and honor will be lost, our children will be taken captive, and our warriors will be killed." [^1] The above verses recount the outcome of this event.

Some believe these verses were revealed before the incident of the Banu Nadir Jews and foretell the future events of this story; for this reason, they consider it among the miraculous reports of the unseen (Ghayb) in the Quran.

Although the tone of the verses, which are mentioned in the present/future tense (Mudari'), supports this view, the connection of these verses with the preceding ones—which were revealed after the defeat and exile of Banu Nadir—indicates that these verses were also revealed after the event, and the use of the present tense is for "vivid portrayal of a past state" (Hikayat al-Hal) (take note).

Commentary

The Role of Hypocrites in the Jewish Seditions

After explaining the story of the Jewish tribe of "Banu Nadir" in the previous verses and describing the state of the three groups of believers—namely the "Muhajirin," the "Ansar," and the "Tabi'un"—with the characteristics of each, the verses under discussion address the state of another group, namely the hypocrites, and their role in this event. This is to make the situation of everyone clearer through comparison, as it is the method of the Quran to introduce groups by placing them in comparison with one another.

First, addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: "Have you not seen those who are hypocrites, saying to their brothers who disbelieved among the People of the Scripture, 'If you are expelled, we will surely leave with you, and we will never obey anyone concerning you; and if you are fought against, we will surely aid you'?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوانِهِمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ وَ لا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَداً أَبَداً وَ إِنْ قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنْصُرَنَّكُمْ).

Thus, this group of hypocrites made three promises to the Jewish tribe, in all of which they were lying: First, that if you are driven out of this land, we will not remain here after you to see your place empty! Second, if an order is issued against you, from whoever or whatever authority it may be, we will never obey it, not now and not ever! Third, if it comes to battle, we will stand shoulder to shoulder with you and will not hesitate for a moment to aid you! Yes, these were the promises the hypocrites gave to the Jews before this event, but subsequent events showed that they were all lies.

For this reason, the Quran explicitly states: "And Allah bears witness that they are liars" (وَ اللَّهُ يَشْهَدُ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ).

What a staggering expression, accompanied by various forms of emphasis! Mentioning Allah as a witness, using a nominal sentence (Jumlah Ismiyyah), and employing "Inna" and the "Lam of emphasis" all show that "lying" and "hypocrisy" are so intertwined that separation between the two is impossible. Hypocrites have always been liars, and liars are often hypocrites.

The expression "their brothers" (إخوانهم) shows that there is a very close relationship and bond between the "hypocrites" and the "disbelievers," just as the previous verses emphasized the bond of brotherhood among the believers. The difference is that believers are sincere in their brotherhood and thus do not withhold any sacrifice or selflessness; conversely, hypocrites have no loyalty or true sympathy, and they abandon their brothers in the most difficult moments. This is the difference between the brotherhood of believers and that of disbelievers.

The phrase "and we will never obey anyone concerning you" (وَ لا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَداً أَبَداً) refers to the fact that they would completely ignore the recommendations, warnings, and notices of Muhammad regarding them.

Then, to further explain their lying, He adds: "If they are expelled, they [the hypocrites] will not leave with them" (لَئِنْ أُخْرِجُوا لا يَخْرُجُونَ مَعَهُمْ). "And if they are fought against, they will not aid them" (وَ لَئِنْ قُوتِلُوا لا يَنْصُرُونَهُمْ).

"And even if they were to act on their word and rise to aid them, they would soon turn their backs and flee!" (وَ لَئِنْ نَصَرُوهُمْ لَيُوَلُّنَّ الْأَدْبارَ). "And then they will not be aided" (ثُمَّ لا يُنْصَرُونَ).

The decisive and crushing tone of these verses sends a shiver through the body of every hypocrite and opponent, especially since the verse, although revealed regarding a specific case, is certainly not limited to it. This is a general principle regarding the relationship between "hypocrites" and "other enemies of Islam," their close cooperation, the promises and threats they give each other, and the groundlessness of all these agreements.

This matter did not only occur in the past history of Islam; today we see living examples of it in the cooperation of hypocrites in Islamic countries with the enemies of Islam, and it will remain true tomorrow and in the future. Certainly, if true believers fulfill their duties, they will prevail over them, and their plans will be thwarted.

In the next verse, He explains the cause of this failure, saying: "You are more intense [as a cause of] fear in their hearts than Allah" (لَأَنْتُمْ أَشَدُّ رَهْبَةً فِي صُدُورِهِمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ). Because they do not fear God, they are terrified of everything, especially of steadfast and believing enemies like you.

"That is because they are a people who do not understand" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ).

"Rahbah" (رهبة) originally means a fear accompanied by anxiety and caution; in reality, it is a deep and rooted fear whose effects appear in action.

Although the above verse was revealed regarding the Banu Nadir Jews and the factors of their defeat against the Muslims, its content is a general and universal rule. This is because two fears can never coexist in the human heart: fear of God and fear of what is besides Allah. Since everything is subject to God's command, anyone who fears God and is aware of His power has no reason to fear anyone else. The source of all these misfortunes is ignorance and the lack of understanding of the reality of Monotheism (Tawhid).

If Muslims today were "Muslim," "Believer," and "Monotheist" in the true sense of the word, not only would they not fear the great military and industrial powers of today's world, but those powers would fear them—as we see living examples where, despite all their advanced weapons and means, they are still in terror of a "small but believing and self-sacrificing nation."

A similar meaning appears in verse 151 of Surah Al-Imran: "We will cast terror into the hearts of those who disbelieve because they have associated with Allah that for which He had not sent down any authority. And their refuge will be the Fire, and wretched is the residence of the wrongdoers!"
