(سَنُلْقِي فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الرُّعْبَ بِما أَشْرَكُوا بِاللَّهِ ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ سُلْطاناً وَ مَأْواهُمُ النَّارُ وَ بِئْسَ مَثْوَى الظَّالِمِينَ).

Then, describing a clear sign of this internal fear, He adds: "They will not fight you all together except within fortified cities or from behind walls; they are terrified of facing you" (لا يُقاتِلُونَكُمْ جَمِيعاً إِلَّا فِي قُرىً مُحَصَّنَةٍ أَوْ مِنْ وَراءِ جُدُرٍ). "Qura" (قرى) is the plural of "Qaryah," meaning a settlement, whether a city or a village, and sometimes it refers to people gathered in one place.

"Muhassanah" (محصنة) is from the root "Hisn" (حصن), meaning a fortress. Therefore, "Qura Muhassanah" refers to settlements that are safe from enemy attack by means of towers, walls, trenches, or other obstacles.

"Judur" (جدر) is the plural of "Jidar" (جدار), meaning a wall, and the original root of this word means height and loftiness.

Yes, because they are outside the fortress of faith and trust in God, they do not dare to fight or face the believers except under the protection of walls and strong castles!

Then He adds: But this is not because they are weak, incapable, or ignorant of the arts of war; "rather, their violence among themselves is severe" (بَأْسُهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ شَدِيدٌ).

But when facing you, the scene changes, and a strange terror, dread, and anxiety dominates them.

Of course, this is also almost a general principle regarding the fighting of all faithless peoples among themselves, and then their fighting against believers. We have repeatedly seen examples of this in contemporary history: when people heedless of God fall upon one another, they strike each other so hard that one does not doubt their combativeness. However, when they stand against a group of believers ready for "martyrdom in the way of Allah," they immediately retreat behind weapons, trenches, and fortified strongholds, and terror overwhelms them. Truly, if Muslims revive and implement faith and Islamic values, they will once again possess such a superior factor against their enemies.

Continuing the same verse, He addresses another factor for their failure and frustration, saying: "You think they are together, but their hearts are diverse. That is because they are a people who do not reason" (تَحْسَبُهُمْ جَمِيعاً وَ قُلُوبُهُمْ شَتَّى ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَعْقِلُونَ).

"Shatta" (شتى) is the plural of "Shatit" (شتيت), meaning "divided" or "scattered."

Truly, the Quran is very precise and inspiring in analyzing issues. It says:

"Internal division and hypocrisy" stem from "ignorance and lack of awareness," because "ignorance" is a factor of polytheism (Shirk), and polytheism is a factor of division, and division causes defeat. Conversely, "knowledge" is the factor of unity (Tawhid) in belief and action, and of agreement and harmony, which in turn is the source of victories.

Thus, the outward cohesion of faithless individuals and their military and economic unity pacts should never deceive us, for behind these pacts and slogans of unity lie scattered hearts. The reason for this is clear: each is protecting his own material interests, and we know that material interests are always in conflict. Meanwhile, the unity and cohesion of believers are based on principles in which conflict has no place—namely, the principle of faith, monotheism, and divine values. Therefore, wherever defeat and failure afflict Muslims, according to the above verses, the reason is that they have distanced themselves from the reality of faith, and their situation will not change until they return.

[^1]: "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 439; the same meaning with some differences appears in "Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 199.

Ayahs

كَمَثَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ قَرِيبًا ۖ ذَاقُوا۟ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٥)
just like those who tasted the evil consequence of their conduct recently before them, and there is a painful punishment for them. (15)
كَمَثَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ إِذْ قَالَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ ٱكْفُرْ فَلَمَّا كَفَرَ قَالَ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦)
[The hypocrites are] like Satan when he tells man to disbelieve, but when he disbelieves, he says, ‘I am absolved of you. Indeed I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds.’ (16)
فَكَانَ عَـٰقِبَتَهُمَآ أَنَّهُمَا فِى ٱلنَّارِ خَـٰلِدَيْنِ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٧)
So the fate of both is that they will be in the Fire, to remain in it [forever]. Such is the requital of the wrongdoers. (17)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٨)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for Tomorrow, and be wary of Allah. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do (18)
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَأَنسَىٰهُمْ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (١٩)
Do not be like those who forget Allah, so He makes them forget their own souls. It is they who are the transgressors. (19)
لَا يَسْتَوِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (٢٠)
Not equal are the inmates of the Fire and the inhabitants of paradise. It is the inhabitants of paradise who are the successful ones. (20)

Commentary

Do Not Go Down the Well with the Frayed Rope of Satan!

These verses continue the discussion regarding the story of the Jewish tribe of Banu al-Nadir and the hypocrites, and through two striking parables, they define the position of each of these two groups:

First, He says: "The story of the Banu al-Nadir is like those who were shortly before them; those who tasted the bitter result of their conduct in this world and shall have a painful punishment in the Hereafter" (كَمَثَلِ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ قَرِيباً ذاقُوا وَبالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ) [^1]. But who were this group that had an exemplary fate before the event of Banu al-Nadir, such that there was not a great distance between these two incidents?

[^1]: This sentence is the predicate (khabar) for an omitted subject (mubtada), and the estimation is: "Their likeness is like the likeness of those before them."

Some consider them to be the polytheists of Mecca who, in the Battle of Badr, tasted the bitter taste of defeat with their entire being, and the strikes of the soldiers of Islam brought them down. This is because the event of "Badr" was not far removed from the affair of "Banu al-Nadir," as the Banu al-Nadir incident—as previously mentioned—occurred after the Battle of Uhud, and the event of Badr took place one year before Uhud; therefore, there was no great distance between these two events.

However, many commentators believe it refers to the story of the Jewish tribe of "Banu Qaynuqa," which occurred after the Battle of Badr and led to the expulsion of this Jewish group from Medina. This interpretation seems more appropriate, as it bears a closer resemblance to the Banu al-Nadir. Like the Banu al-Nadir, the Banu Qaynuqa were wealthy, arrogant, and warriors among themselves, and they threatened the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims with their power and strength—as we shall detail in the "Key Points," God willing—but ultimately gained nothing but misery and displacement in this world and a painful punishment in the Hereafter.

"Wabal" (وبال) means an evil and bitter consequence. Originally, it is derived from "Wabil" (وابل), meaning heavy rain, because heavy rains are usually fearsome, and man dreads their bitter end as they often result in dangerous floods.

Then, He presents a parable regarding the hypocrites, saying: "Their story is also like the story of Satan when he said to man: 'Disbelieve,' so that I may solve your problems; but when he disbelieved, he said: 'I am free of you; I fear Allah, the Lord of the Worlds'" (كَمَثَلِ الشَّيْطانِ إِذْ قالَ لِلْإِنْسانِ اكْفُرْ فَلَمَّا كَفَرَ قالَ إِنِّي بَرِي‌ءٌ مِنْكَ إِنِّي أَخافُ اللَّهَ رَبَّ الْعالَمِينَ) [^1]. Regarding who is meant by "man" (al-insan) in this verse: Is it mankind in general who fall under the influence of Satan, are deceived by his false promises, and tread the path of disbelief, only for Satan to abandon them and disassociate himself from them?

Or does it refer to a specific person, such as "Abu Jahl" and his followers, who were encouraged by the deceptive promises of Satan during the Battle of Badr and ultimately tasted the bitter taste of defeat? As we read in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 48: "And remember when Satan made their deeds seem fair to them and said: 'No one can overcome you today from among the people, and I am your protector.' But when the two armies came in sight of each other, he turned on his heels and said: 'I am free of you; I see what you do not see; I fear Allah, and Allah is severe in punishment!'" Or is the "man" meant here "Barsisa," the worshiper from the Children of Israel, who was deceived by Satan and disbelieved, and at the critical moment, Satan disassociated himself from him and left him? The details of this will come, God willing.

[^1]: Although the expression "kamathal" (like the likeness) in this verse and the previous one are similar—which is why some commentators consider both to refer to the same group—the context clearly testifies that the first refers to the state of the Banu al-Nadir Jews, and the second refers to the state of the hypocrites. In any case, this phrase is also the predicate of an omitted subject, estimated as: "Their likeness is like the likeness of Satan..."

However, the first interpretation is more consistent with the general concept of the verse, while the second and third interpretations can be seen as specific instances of that broad concept. In any case, the punishment that Satan expresses fear of is apparently the punishment of this world; therefore, his fear is serious, not a joke or mockery. There are many who fear immediate punishments but are indifferent to long-term ones.

Indeed, such is the state of the hypocrites who send their friends into the heart of the fray with false promises and trickery, then leave them alone and flee, for there is no loyalty in hypocrisy.

In the next verse, He clarifies the end of these two groups: "Satan and his followers" and "the hypocrites and their friends from the people of disbelief," adding: "The end of both of them is that they will be in the Fire, abiding eternally therein; and that is the recompense of the wrongdoers" (فَكانَ عاقِبَتَهُما أَنَّهُما فِي النَّارِ خالِدَيْنِ فِيها وَ ذلِكَ جَزاءُ الظَّالِمِينَ) [^1]. This is a general principle: the end of the cooperation between disbelief and hypocrisy, and Satan and his allies, is defeat, failure, and punishment in this world and the Hereafter. In contrast, the cooperation of the believers and their friends is a continuous and eternal cooperation, and its end is victory and the enjoyment of Allah's vast mercy in both worlds.

In the following verse, the address is turned toward the believers as a conclusion drawn from the grim and painful story of the "Banu al-Nadir," the hypocrites, and Satan, saying: "O you who have believed! Fear Allah, and let every soul look to what it has sent forth for tomorrow" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ لْتَنْظُرْ نَفْسٌ ما قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ) [^1]. Then, He adds once more for emphasis: "And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Aware of what you do" (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ).

Indeed, piety (taqwa) and the fear of Allah cause a person to think about the tomorrow of the Resurrection and to make his deeds pure, clean, and sincere.

The repetition of the command for piety, as we said, is for emphasis, because the motivation for all righteous deeds and the avoidance of sin is this very piety and fear of God.

However, some have suggested that the first command to piety refers to the essence of performing deeds, and the second to the quality of their sincerity. Or that the first refers to performing good deeds (indicated by the phrase "what it has sent forth for tomorrow") and the second refers to abstaining from sins and transgressions. Or that the first refers to repentance for past sins and the second to piety for the future. But there is no specific evidence in the verses for these interpretations, and emphasis seems more appropriate.

The expression "tomorrow" (ghad) refers to the Day of Resurrection, because relative to the scale of the world's lifespan, it arrives quickly, and its mention in an indefinite form (nakirah) is to highlight its importance.

The expression "a soul" (nafs) here may mean "every single person," meaning every human must think of his own tomorrow, and without expecting others to do something for him, he should send forth what he can while he is still in this world.

[^1]: Regarding whether "ma" in "ma qaddamat li-ghad" is relative (mawsulah) or interrogative (istifhamiyah), commentators have offered two possibilities, and the noble verse can accommodate both, though the interrogative seems more fitting.

Another interpretation regarding the above expression is that it refers to the scarcity of individuals who think about the tomorrow of the Resurrection—as if we say, "Let there be even one person who thinks of his own salvation." However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate, and the address "O you who have believed" and the "generality of the command to piety" are evidence for the general nature of the verse's meaning.

Following the command to piety and attention to the Hereafter, the next verse emphasizes the remembrance of Allah, stating: "And be not like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget themselves" (وَ لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَأَنْساهُمْ أَنْفُسَهُمْ). Fundamentally, the essence of piety consists of two things: the remembrance of Allah—meaning attention to His constant supervision and His presence everywhere and at all times—and attention to Allah's court of justice and the record of deeds in which no small or large action exists except that it is recorded. For this reason, attention to these two principles (the Origin and the Return) has been at the forefront of the educational programs of the Prophets and the Saints, and its impact on the purification of the individual and society is completely remarkable.

It is noteworthy that the Quran here explicitly states that forgetting Allah leads to "self-forgetfulness." The reason for this is also clear: on one hand, forgetting the Lord causes a person to sink into material pleasures and animalistic desires, forgetting the purpose of his creation, and consequently becoming negligent of the necessary provisions for the tomorrow of the Resurrection.

On the other hand, forgetting Allah is accompanied by forgetting His pure attributes—that He is Absolute Existence, Infinite Knowledge, and Boundless Self-Sufficiency—and that everything other than Him is dependent on Him and in need of His Pure Essence. This very matter causes man to consider himself independent, wealthy, and self-sufficient, and thus forget his own human reality and identity [^1]. Fundamentally, one of the greatest misfortunes and tragedies for man is self-forgetfulness, because he consigns to oblivion his inherent values, talents, and merits that God has placed within him and by which He distinguished him from the rest of creation. This is equivalent to forgetting his own humanity, and such a person falls to the level of a predatory animal, and his ambition will be nothing but sleep, food, and lust! All of these are the primary factors of immorality (fisq), rather, this self-forgetfulness is the worst manifestation of immorality and exiting from the obedience of Allah. For this reason, at the end of the verse, it says:

"Such people are the defiantly disobedient" (أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ).

It is also noteworthy that it does not say "do not forget Allah," but rather says: "be not like those who forgot Allah and He made them forget themselves." This, in fact, shows a clear, perceptible example in which they can see the consequence of forgetting Allah.

This verse apparently refers to the hypocrites mentioned in the previous verses, or the Banu al-Nadir Jews, or both.

A similar meaning appears in verse 67 of Surah At-Tawbah specifically regarding the hypocrites, where it says: "The hypocrite men and hypocrite women are of one another. They enjoin what is wrong and forbid what is right and close their hands. They have forgotten Allah, so He has forgotten them [of His mercy]. Indeed, the hypocrites are the defiantly disobedient."

The difference is that there, forgetting Allah is mentioned as the cause for the cutting off of His mercy, and here as the cause for self-forgetfulness, both of which lead to the same point (reflect on this).

In the final verse under discussion, it compares these two groups (the group of pious believers who are mindful of the Origin and the Return, and the group of those who forgot Allah and were afflicted with self-forgetfulness), saying: "The companions of the Fire and the companions of Paradise are not equal" (لا يَسْتَوِي أَصْحابُ النَّارِ وَ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ). Neither in this world, nor in knowledge, nor in the way of thinking, nor in the manner of individual and collective life and its goal, nor in the Hereafter and divine rewards. The path of these two groups is separate everywhere and in everything: one is mindful of Allah, the Resurrection, the revival of high human values, and the amassing of provisions for eternal life; the other is drowned in lusts and material pleasures, afflicted with forgetting everything, and a prisoner to the bonds of whim and desire [^1]. Thus, man stands at a crossroads: he must either join the first group or the second, and there is no third way.

And at the end of the verse, it states as a decisive ruling: "Only the companions of Paradise are the attainers [of success]" (أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ هُمُ الْفائِزُونَ).

Not only are they successful and victorious in the Resurrection, but in this world too, victory, peace, and salvation belong to them, while defeat in both worlds is the lot of those who forget.

[^1]: The omission of the object (related to "are not equal") is evidence of generality.

In a hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he interpreted "the companions of Paradise" as those who obeyed him and accepted the Wilayah (guardianship) of Ali عليه السلام, and "the companions of the Fire" as those who disliked the Wilayah of Ali عليه السلام, broke the covenant, and fought against him [^1]. Of course, this is one of the clear instances of the verse's meaning and does not diminish the generality of the verse's concept.

Key Points

1- Cooperation with the People of Hypocrisy Leads Nowhere!

What has been mentioned in the above verses regarding the hypocrites' breaking of promises and abandoning their friends in difficult and critical moments is something we have seen examples of many times in our lives.

Like the seductive Satan, they engage in whispering to this person and that, giving them all sorts of promises of assistance and help, sending them into the field of events and tainting them with various sins. But in the most crisis-ridden states, they leave them in the middle of the field and flee to preserve their own lives or interests! This is the fate of those who are collaborators and allies with the hypocrites.

A living example of this in our era is the treaties that the great powers and the satans of our time sign with the heads of states dependent on them. We have seen many times that these dependent governments, which have placed everything they have on the tray of sincerity and sacrificed it for those satanic protectors, are left completely alone in hard times and are driven away from everywhere. It is here that we become more familiar with the depth of the Quranic message which says:

"Like the likeness of Satan when he said to man: 'Disbelieve,' and when he disbelieved, he said: 'I am free of you; I fear Allah, the Lord of the Worlds'!"

2- The Astonishing Story of Barsisa the Worshiper

Some commentators and masters of hadith have narrated a meaningful tradition under these verses about a worshiper from the Children of Israel named "Barsisa," which can be a great lesson for everyone never to go down a well with the frayed rope of Satan and the hypocrites—for going down is one thing, and being overturned at the bottom of the well is another! The summary of the story is as follows:

Among the Children of Israel was a worshiper named "Barsisa" who had worshiped for a long time and had reached such a level of proximity that the mentally ill were brought to him, and through his prayer, they would regain their health. One day, a young woman from a prominent family was brought to him by her brothers, and it was decided she would stay for a while to be healed. Here, Satan began his whispering and beautified the scene in his eyes to such an extent that the worshiper violated her! Soon it became clear that the woman was pregnant (and since sin is always the source of greater sins), he murdered the woman and buried her in a corner of the desert! Her brothers found out that the worshiper had committed such a horrific crime; the news spread throughout the city and reached the ears of the ruler. He set out with a group of people to investigate. When the worshiper's crimes were proven, they pulled him down from his place of worship. After he confessed to the sin, the ruler ordered him to be hanged. When he was placed upon the gallows, Satan manifested before him and said: "It was I who brought you to this state! And if you obey what I say, I will provide the means for your rescue!" The worshiper said: "What should I do?" He said: "Just one prostration to me is enough!" The worshiper said: "In this state you see, I have no power." Satan said: "A gesture is sufficient." The worshiper made a gesture with the corner of his eye or his hand and prostrated to Satan, and instantly died and left the world as a disbeliever! [^1] Indeed, such is the end of the whisperings of the satans and the hypocrites who follow their line.

3- What Must Be Sent Forth

In the above verses, emphasis was placed on the fact that man must look to see which provision he has sent forth for his tomorrow of the Resurrection: "وَ لْتَنْظُرْ نَفْسٌ ما قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ". In reality, man's primary capital on the scene of the Resurrection is the deeds he has sent forth; otherwise, usually no one thinks of a person to send something for him after his death, or if they do send something, it does not have much value.

Therefore, in a hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "Spend in the way of Allah, even if it is the size of a 'man' (a measure) of dates or less, or a handful or less, or even half a date! And if one does not find even that, let him gladden hearts with pure words. For on the Day of Resurrection, when you stand in the presence of Allah, He will say to you: 'Did I not do such and such for you? Did I not place hearing and sight at your disposal? Did I not grant you wealth and children?' And the servant will say: 'Yes.' At this point, Allah Almighty says: 'Then look and see what you have sent forth for yourself from before?' He will look in front of him, behind him, to his right, and to his left, and find nothing with which to protect his face from the Fire!" [^1]

In another hadith, we read: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was sitting with some of his companions when a group from the tribe of "Mudar" entered, wearing swords at their waists (ready for Jihad in the way of Allah), but they did not have proper clothing on their bodies. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw the signs of need and hunger on their faces, the color of his face changed. He came to the mosque, ascended the pulpit, performed the praise and glorification of Allah, and said: "Allah has revealed this verse in the Holy Quran: 'O you who have believed! Fear Allah, and let every soul look to what it has sent forth for tomorrow...'" Then he added: "Spend in the way of Allah before power is taken from you, and give charity in the way of Allah before an obstacle is created in this path. Those who have dinars from their dinars, those who have dirhams from their dirhams, and those who have wheat and barley from their wheat and barley. Do not belittle anything of spending, even if it is half a date."

A man from the Ansar stood up and placed a bag in the blessed hand of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; signs of happiness and joy appeared on the face of the Prophet. He said: "Whoever establishes a good tradition (sunnah hasanah) and people act upon it, its reward and the reward of all those who act upon it will be his, without anything being diminished from their rewards. And whoever establishes an evil tradition, its sin and the sin of all those who act upon it will be upon him, without anything being diminished from their sins." People stood up; he who had dinars brought dinars, he who had dirhams brought dirhams, and whoever had food or anything else brought it to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In this way, a significant amount of monetary and non-monetary aid was collected before the Prophet and distributed among those needy people [^1].

This same meaning has been repeatedly emphasized in other verses of the Quran, including verse 110 of Surah Al-Baqarah, where we read: "And establish prayer and give zakah, and whatever good you send forth for yourselves, you will find it with Allah. Indeed, Allah is Seeing of what you do."

[^1]: Tafsir "Durr al-Manthur," vol. 6, p. 201.

Ayahs

لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَّرَأَيْتَهُۥ خَـٰشِعًا مُّتَصَدِّعًا مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢١)
Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled [and] go to pieces with the fear of Allah. We draw such comparisons for mankind, so that they may reflect. (21)
هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ۖ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٢٢)
He is Allah—there is no god except Him—Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, He is the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (22)
هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٢٣)
He is Allah—there is no god except Him—the Sovereign, the All-holy, the All-benign, the Securer, the All-conserver, the All-mighty, the All-compeller, and the All-magnanimous. Clear is Allah of any partners that they may ascribe [to Him]! (23)
هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٤)
He is Allah, the Creator, the Maker, and the Former. To Him belong the Best Names. Whatever there is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Him and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (24)

Commentary

If the Quran were revealed upon the mountains, they would have split asunder!

Following the previous verses, which utilized various methods to penetrate human hearts and described the life-altering issues of humanity in their most vivid form, these verses—which are the final verses of Surah al-Hashr and pertain to all the verses of the Holy Quran—unveil the reality that the influence of the Quran is so deep that if it were revealed upon the mountains, it would shake them. Yet, how strange is this hard-hearted human who sometimes hears it and remains unmoved! First, He says: "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have surely seen it humbled and splitting asunder from the fear of Allah" (لَوْ أَنْزَلْنا هذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلى‌ جَبَلٍ لَرَأَيْتَهُ خاشِعاً مُتَصَدِّعاً مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ‌).

"And these are the examples We set forth for mankind so that they may reflect" (وَ تِلْكَ الْأَمْثالُ نَضْرِبُها لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ‌).

Many commentators have interpreted this verse as a simile, stating that the objective is that these mountains, despite all their solidity and strength, if they possessed intellect and feeling and these verses were revealed upon them instead of human hearts, they would tremble so violently that they would split apart. However, a group of cruel and hard-hearted humans hear it, and the slightest change does not occur within them (they take the phrase "And these are the examples We set forth for mankind" as evidence for this interpretation).

Others have taken it literally, saying: All beings in this world, including mountains, possess a type of perception and consciousness, and if these verses were revealed to them, they would truly disintegrate. They consider verse 74 of Surah al-Baqarah as evidence for this meaning, which says in describing a group of Jews:

ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ فَهِيَ كَالْحِجارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً وَ إِنَّ مِنَ الْحِجارَةِ لَما يَتَفَجَّرُ مِنْهُ الْأَنْهارُ وَ إِنَّ مِنْها لَما يَشَّقَّقُ فَيَخْرُجُ مِنْهُ الْماءُ وَ إِنَّ مِنْها لَما يَهْبِطُ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ‌:

"Then your hearts became hardened after that, being like stones or even harder. For indeed, there are stones from which rivers burst forth, and there are some of them that split open and water comes out from them, and there are some of them that fall down for fear of Allah." The term "mathal" (example/parable) may mean "description," as this word has appeared repeatedly in the Holy Quran with this meaning; therefore, the aforementioned expression does not contradict this interpretation.

It is noteworthy that He first says: the mountains would become humble and submissive before the Quran, and then He adds: they would split asunder. This indicates that the Quran would penetrate them gradually, and at every moment, new signs of the Quran's influence would appear in them until they lost their endurance and became like a restless, infatuated lover, and then they would split apart. [^1]

In the subsequent verses, He mentions a significant portion of the attributes of Allah's Beauty and Majesty, the contemplation of each having a profound effect on the cultivation of souls and the refinement of hearts. Within three verses, He enumerates 15 attributes—or by another count, 18 attributes—of His Great Attributes. Each verse begins with the declaration of Divine Oneness (Tawhid) and the sacred name "Allah," guiding man toward the luminous world of the Names and Attributes of the Truth.

He says: "He is Allah, besides whom there is no deity, Knower of the unseen and the seen. He is the Most Compassionate, the Most Merciful" (هُوَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ هُوَ الرَّحْمنُ الرَّحِيمُ‌).

Here, before anything else, He emphasizes the issue of Tawhid, which is the essence of all attributes of Beauty and Majesty and the primary root of Divine Gnosis, and after that, He emphasizes His knowledge regarding the unseen and the seen.

"Shahadah" and "Shuhud"—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—is presence coupled with observation, whether with the physical eye or the eye of the heart. Therefore, wherever the realm of human sensory and scientific encompassment lies is the "world of the seen" (Alam al-Shahadah), and whatever is outside this realm is considered the "world of the unseen" (Alam al-Ghayb). However, all of these are equal before the knowledge of Allah, for His infinite existence is present and observant everywhere; thus, no place is outside the realm of His knowledge and presence.

[^1]: "Mutasaddi'" is from the root "sad'" meaning the splitting of hard and firm objects, such as iron and glass. If a headache is called "suda'," it is because it feels as if it wants to split one's head apart.

Hence, in verse 59 of Surah al-An'am, we read: وَ عِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ: "And with Him are the keys of the unseen; none knows them except Him."

Mindfulness of this Divine Name causes man to consider Him present and observant everywhere and to adopt God-wariness (Taqwa).

Then, emphasis is placed on His general mercy which encompasses all creatures (ar-Rahman) and His specific mercy which is exclusive to the believers (ar-Rahim), in order to give hope to man and to assist him on the long path of perfection and the journey toward Allah that lies ahead; for traversing this stage is impossible without the accompaniment of His grace, as it is darkness and carries the risk of going astray.

In this way, in addition to the attribute of Tawhid, three of His great attributes are mentioned in this verse, each of which is inspiring in its own way.

In the next verse, in addition to emphasizing the issue of Tawhid, He mentions eight other attributes, saying: "He is Allah, besides whom there is no deity" (هُوَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ).

"The Sovereign (al-Malik)"—He is the absolute Ruler and Owner.

"The Holy (al-Quddus)"—He is pure and free from every defect and flaw.

"The Source of Peace (as-Salam)"—He allows no injustice or oppression toward anyone, and all are in safety from His side. [^1]

[^1]: Some commentators have interpreted "Salam" here to mean being free from every defect, flaw, and blight. However, considering that this meaning is included in the word "Quddus" which preceded it, and furthermore, "Salam" in the Holy Quran usually refers to the issue of granting safety to others, and fundamentally the word "Salam" spoken to another upon meeting means expressing friendship and healthy relations with the other party, what we mentioned above seems more appropriate (reflect on this).

Fundamentally, His call is toward peace: "And Allah invites to the Home of Peace" (Yunus: 25), and His guidance is also directed toward peace: "Allah guides by it those who follow His pleasure to the ways of peace" (al-Ma'idah: 16). The abode He has prepared for the believers is also the Home of Peace: "For them is the Home of Peace with their Lord." The greeting of the inhabitants of Paradise is also nothing but "Peace": "Except the saying: Peace, Peace" (al-Waqi'ah: 26).

Then He adds: "The Granter of Security (al-Mu'min)"—He grants security to His friends and bestows faith. [^1] "The Overseer (al-Muhaymin)"—He is the Protector, Preserver, and Watcher over all things. [^2] "The All-Mighty (al-Aziz)"—He is the Powerful One who is never defeated.

"The Compeller (al-Jabbar)."

This term, derived from the root "jabr," sometimes means coercion, dominance, and the enforcement of will, and sometimes it means compensation and restoration. Raghib in Al-Mufradat combines both meanings, saying: the root of jabr is to rectify something through dominance and power. When this term is used regarding Allah, it expresses one of His great attributes: that through the enforcement of His will and perfection of power, He rectifies every corruption. Whenever it is used regarding other than Him, it carries a sense of blame, and according to Raghib, it is said of one who wishes to "compensate" for his own deficiency and lack by claiming statuses he does not deserve. This term is used ten times in the Holy Quran, nine of which refer to oppressive, rebellious, and corrupt individuals, and only one instance refers to Allah the Almighty (the verse under discussion).

[^1]: Some commentators have interpreted "Mu'min" here to mean "the Possessor of Faith," referring to the fact that the first one who has faith in the Pure Essence of Allah, His attributes, and His messengers is He Himself. However, what we mentioned above is more appropriate.

[^2]: Regarding the origin of this word, there are two opinions among commentators and philologists. Some consider it from the root "haymana," which means watching, protecting, and preserving. Others consider it from the root "iman," where the hamza has been changed to "ha," meaning to grant tranquility. This word is used twice in the Holy Quran: once regarding the Quran itself (al-Ma'idah: 48) and once in describing Allah in the verse under discussion; in both cases, the first meaning is appropriate (Lisan al-Arab, Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, and Ruh al-Ma'ani). Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, under the verse in question, narrates from Abu Ubaydah that in the Arabic language, there are only five names on this weight (pattern): Muhaymin, Musaytir (dominant), Mubaytir (veterinarian), Mubayqir (one who opens his way and moves forward), and Mukhaymir (the name of a mountain).

Then He adds: "The Superior (al-Mutakabbir)"—He is worthy of greatness, and there is nothing higher or greater than Him.

"Mutakabbir" from the root "takabbur" comes with two meanings: one is praiseworthy, which is used regarding Allah, meaning the possession of greatness, good deeds, and many commendable attributes. The other is blameworthy and condemned, used regarding other than Allah, which is when small and insignificant individuals claim greatness and attribute to themselves qualities they do not possess. Since majesty and greatness are worthy only of the station of Allah, this term in its praiseworthy sense is used only for Him, and whenever it is used for other than Him, it is in the blameworthy sense.

At the end of the verse, He once again emphasizes the issue of Tawhid, with which the discourse began, saying: "Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him" (سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ‌).

With the explanation provided, it becomes clear that no being can be a partner, likeness, or peer to Him in the attributes mentioned here.

In the final verse under discussion, to complete these attributes, He mentions six other descriptions, saying: "He is Allah, the Creator (al-Khaliq)."

"The Producer (al-Bari')"—the God who brought creatures into existence without flaw and without a prior likeness. [^1] "The Fashioner (al-Musawwir)"—the Creator who gave each being a specific form.

Then, since the attributes of Allah are not limited to these descriptions—rather, His attributes, like His Essence, are infinite—He adds: "To Him belong the Best Names" (لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى‌).

For this reason, He is sanctified and free from every defect and flaw, "and whatever is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Him," considering Him pure from every fault and deficiency (يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

Finally, for further emphasis on the system of creation, He refers to two more of His attributes, one of which appeared previously, saying: "And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ‌).

The first is a sign of the perfection of His power over all things and His dominance over every obstacle, and the second refers to His knowledge and awareness of the system of creation and the arrangement of a precise plan in the matter of creation and governance.

[^1]: Bari' is from the root bar' (on the pattern of qufl), which originally means recovery and release from unpleasant matters. Therefore, Bari' is said of one who creates something without flaw and perfectly proportioned. Some also take it from the root bara (on the pattern of nafy) meaning to carve wood, which is also done to make it proportioned. Some philologists have also specified that Bari' is one who initiates something without precedent and without a prior model.

Thus, in the total of these three verses, in addition to the issue of Tawhid which is repeated twice, seventeen attributes of Allah's attributes are mentioned as follows:

  1. Knower of the Unseen and the Seen.
  2. The Most Compassionate (ar-Rahman).
  3. The Most Merciful (ar-Rahim).
  4. The Sovereign (al-Malik).
  5. The Holy (al-Quddus).
  6. The Source of Peace (as-Salam).
  7. The Granter of Security (al-Mu'min).
  8. The Overseer (al-Muhaymin).
  9. The All-Mighty (al-Aziz).
  10. The Compeller (al-Jabbar).
  11. The Superior (al-Mutakabbir).
  12. The Creator (al-Khaliq).
  13. The Producer (al-Bari').
  14. The Fashioner (al-Musawwir).
  15. The All-Wise (al-Hakim).
  16. Possessor of the Best Names (al-Asma al-Husna).
  17. The One whom all beings in the universe glorify.

Including the attribute of His Oneness (Tawhid), the total becomes eighteen attributes (note that Tawhid is mentioned twice and al-Aziz is also mentioned twice).

Among the total of these attributes, a specific type of systematic arrangement is seen across the three verses:

In the first verse, He discusses the most general attributes of the Essence (Knowledge) and the most general attributes of action (Mercy), which is the root of all Divine actions.

In the second verse, the talk is of His sovereignty and the affairs of this sovereignty; attributes such as "al-Quddus," "as-Salam," "al-Mu'min," "al-Jabbar," and "al-Mutakabbir," considering their meanings mentioned above, are all characteristics of this absolute Divine sovereignty.

In the final verse, He discusses the issue of creation and what pertains to it, such as arrangement, fashioning, power, and wisdom.

In this way, these verses take the hand of the seekers of the path of Gnosis of Allah and lead them station by station. It begins from His Pure Essence, then brings them to the world of creation, and again in this journey toward Allah, it leads from the creature back to the Creator. It makes the heart a sanctuary for Divine Names and Attributes and a center for Lordly lights, and amidst these cognitions and lights, it builds and cultivates the person, causing the blossoms of Taqwa to appear on the branches of his existence and making him worthy of His proximity, so that he may harmonize with all the particles of the world and, while glorifying, sing the melody of "Subbuhun Quddus" (All-Glorious, All-Holy).

Therefore, it is not surprising that in Islamic narrations, extraordinary importance has been given to these verses, which will be mentioned in the Key Points, Allah willing.

Key Points

1. The Extraordinary Influence of the Quran

The influence of the Quran on hearts and thoughts is an undeniable reality, and throughout the history of Islam, many evidences of this have been seen. It has been practically proven that the hardest of hearts, upon hearing a few verses, would become so soft that they would suddenly surrender themselves to Islam. Only the stubborn and antagonistic individuals were exceptions to this—those who had no place for guidance within their being.

Thus, we read in the verses above where He says: If this Quran were revealed upon the mountains, they would have been humbled and split asunder, and all of these are signs of the attraction of this Divine Word, which we also feel when reciting it with presence of heart.

2. The Greatness of the Final Verses of Surah al-Hashr

The final verses of this Surah, which contain a significant portion of the Divine Names and Attributes, are verses of extraordinary greatness and inspiration. They are a great lesson of cultivation for humans because they tell them: If you seek proximity to Allah and desire greatness and perfection, kindle a flame of these attributes within your own being.

In some narrations, it is stated that the Greatest Name of Allah (Ism al-A'zam) is in the final verses of Surah al-Hashr. [^1] In another hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Whoever recites the end of al-Hashr, his past and future sins will be forgiven!" [^2] And again in another hadith from him:

"Whoever recites 'Had We sent down this Quran...' until the end—and dies that night, he dies a martyr!" [^3] One of the companions says: I asked the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله about the Greatest Name of Allah, he said:

"You must recite the end of al-Hashr and recite it often!" I repeated the same question again, and the Prophet repeated the same answer. [^4]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, vol. 9, p. 267. [^2], [^3], [^4]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 5, p. 293.

It is even mentioned in a hadith:

"Indeed, they are a cure for every disease except sam, and sam is death!" [^1] In short: "Narrations in this regard are numerous in the books of both Shi'a and Sunni, all of which indicate the greatness of these verses and the necessity of reflection and thought upon their content."

It is noteworthy that just as this Surah begins with the glorification of Allah and the names "al-Aziz" and "al-Hakim," it also ends with the names "al-Aziz" and "al-Hakim," because the ultimate goal of the Surah is the knowledge of Allah, His glorification, and familiarity with His sacred Names and Attributes.

Regarding the "Best Names" (al-Asma al-Husna) mentioned in the verses above, a detailed discussion was provided under verse 180 of Surah al-A'raf. [^2]

O Allah! We swear by the greatness of Your Names and Attributes, make our hearts humble and submissive before the Holy Quran. O Lord! The snare of Satan is firm, and if Your grace does not assist, escaping it is difficult; keep us in the sanctuary of Your grace from the whispers of the devils. O God! Grant us the spirit of sacrifice, Taqwa, and detachment from every kind of stinginess, malice, and envy, and keep us away from selfishness and conceit.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. (End of Surah al-Hashr) (And end of Volume 23) End of Sha'ban 1406 / Ordibehesht 20, 1365 Solar Hijri.
